title: Event handlers

publication.changeSummary: Technology-specific React: Component-Based Browser Applications draft for Event handlers; independent standards, browser, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: React Learn and Reference; React 19.2; provisions applicable to Event handlers
sources.claim: React event handlers respond to user actions and are the primary place to calculate state updates and invoke action-side effects.

sources.locator: Web Content Accessibility Guidelines 2.2; W3C Recommendation 2023; provisions applicable to Event handlers
sources.claim: React event handlers respond to user actions and are the primary place to calculate state updates and invoke action-side effects.

canonicalExplanation.summary: React event handlers respond to user actions and are the primary place to calculate state updates and invoke action-side effects.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: onClick={save} passes the function while onClick={save()} would execute during render. Your goal is to implement keyboard- and pointer-compatible interactions with correct handler arguments. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Props and composition; javascript events model; useState and snapshots. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Technology rule
sections.body: React event handlers respond to user actions and are the primary place to calculate state updates and invoke action-side effects. Apply this rule to the actual markup, stylesheet, JavaScript, or component state rather than memorizing its name.

sections.heading: Boundary and browser reality
sections.body: Handlers must be passed rather than called during render, async handlers capture snapshots, and propagation follows DOM semantics. This draft therefore rejects: Event handler props should call the function immediately so React knows what to do later.

includes: Pass handlers rather than calling them during render and preserve native element semantics.
Implement keyboard- and pointer-compatible interactions with correct handler arguments.
excludes: Handlers must be passed rather than called during render, async handlers capture snapshots, and propagation follows DOM semantics.

outcomes: Implement keyboard- and pointer-compatible interactions with correct handler arguments.

examples.title: Event handlers: concrete case
examples.situation: onClick={save} passes the function while onClick={save()} would execute during render.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Inspect the exact artifact and starting state: onClick={save} passes the function while onClick={save()} would execute during render.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the correction: Pass a function to an event prop; invoking it during render performs the action at the wrong time.
examples.result: The result follows because Pass a function to an event prop; invoking it during render performs the action at the wrong time.

examples.title: Event handlers: boundary case

Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Reject the misconception: Event handler props should call the function immediately so React knows what to do later.
